title: The Stuttering Training Loop
scenario: You're training a small neural network on a GPU, but you notice the GPU utilization is low (e.g., 30-40%), and the training progress seems to 'stutter' periodically, despite using a small dataset that easily fits into GPU memory. What's a common, non-obvious reason for this behavior?

title: The Beam Search Memory Explosion
scenario: You switch your generation algorithm from Greedy Search to Beam Search with a beam width of 4 to get better translation quality. Suddenly, your inference server can only handle 1/4th the number of concurrent users before OOMing. Why does Beam Search destroy your concurrency scaling?

title: The VRAM Budget
scenario: Your team wants to load a 13B-parameter LLM onto a single GPU for inference. The GPU has 24 GB of VRAM. They plan to load the model in FP16. Will it fit? Show your math.

title: The PCIe Bottleneck
scenario: You need to load a 7B model (14 GB in FP16) from CPU RAM to GPU VRAM over PCIe Gen4 x16. How long does the transfer take? Your serving SLA requires cold-start latency under 5 seconds. Will you meet it?

title: The KV-Cache Explosion
scenario: You're serving a 70B LLM with 128k context length on 8× H100 GPUs (80 GB each, 640 GB total). The model weights in FP16 are 140 GB. Your product manager asks 'how many concurrent 128k-context users can we serve?' Calculate the answer.

title: The VRAM Budget
scenario: Your PM asks: 'Can we run Llama-2-7B inference on a single A100 40 GB?' Give me the exact memory breakdown — don't just say 'it fits.'

title: The Sequence Length Trap
scenario: You need to increase your LLM's context window from 4k to 128k tokens. The model weights fit perfectly in your 80GB VRAM. What hidden memory cost will cause your node to OOM (Out of Memory) during generation?

title: The HBM3e Bandwidth Ceiling
scenario: Your team just got a cluster of H200 GPUs with 4.8 TB/s HBM3e, up from the H100's 3.35 TB/s. Your manager expects a 43% speedup across all workloads. After benchmarking, some models got the full speedup while others saw zero improvement. Which workloads actually benefit from the extra bandwidth, and

title: The NUMA Penalty
scenario: You have a dual-socket server with two AMD EPYC CPUs. Each socket has 256 GB of DDR5 RAM and one A100 GPU attached via PCIe. scenario: You're training a 30B model on 8× A100-80GB GPUs. With full activation storage, you OOM at batch size 2. scenario: You're running a 13B LLM serving endpoint. With the naive KV-cache implementation, you can serve 12 concurrent requests before OOMing on an A100-80GB. After switching to vLLM with PagedAttention, you can serve 48 concurrent requests. The model weights didn't change. Where did the extra memory come f

title: The Cache Line Waste
scenario: You have a tensor of shape $[1024, 3]$ stored in row-major (C-contiguous) FP32 format. You iterate over it column-by-column (reading all 1024 elements of column 0, then column 1, etc.). The CPU cache line is 64 bytes. What fraction of each cache line fetch is wasted, and how does this compare to row

title: The Gradient Checkpoint Trade-off
scenario: You're training a 32-layer transformer and running out of memory. Your colleague suggests gradient checkpointing. How much memory does it save, and what's the compute cost? Calculate both for checkpointing every $k$ layers.

title: The Striding Stumble
scenario: You're comparing two C++ implementations of a matrix multiplication kernel on a CPU. Both use standard nested loops and perform the exact same arithmetic operations. However, one is consistently 3-5x slower than the other. Upon inspection, you notice the inner loop access patterns are `matrix[i][j]`

title: The Prompt Caching Optimization
scenario: Every request to our customer-support LLM starts with the same 2,000-token system prompt. We're serving 1,000 requests/minute on 4× H100 GPUs. An engineer says 'we should cache the system prompt's KV-cache.' Quantify the savings — is this worth the engineering effort?

title: The KV-Cache Swap Thrashing
scenario: To handle a massive spike in traffic, you configure vLLM to allow swapping inactive KV-cache blocks to the host CPU RAM via PCIe. This prevents OOM errors. However, you notice that while throughput remains okay, the latency (Time-Per-Output-Token) for individual users randomly spikes from 50ms to 90

scenario: Your team is serving Llama-2-70B. On NVIDIA H100 80 GB, you need 2-way tensor parallelism — the 140 GB of FP16 weights simply don't fit on one GPU. scenario: Your team runs a 13B model on A100 80 GB GPUs for long-running inference sessions. The model weights have

title: The Energy-Movement Invariant
scenario: We pruned 50% of the weights from our model, cutting the total MAC (Multiply-Accumulate) operations in half. However, the energy consumption of the node barely dropped. Why?

scenario: You're scaling up a gradient boosting model training job on a powerful dual-socket CPU server (e.g., two Intel Xeon CPUs). scenario: Your team is designing a custom ML accelerator optimized for large Transformer models with exceptionally long context windows. scenario: Our vLLM instance on an A100-80GB has been serving requests for 6 hours. `nvidia-smi` shows 16 GB used, 64 GB free. But the server rejects new requests with 'Cannot allocate KV-cache.' We have 80% free memory — where did it go?

scenario: We run a recommendation model with a 1 TB embedding table (4 billion entries × 128 dimensions × FP16). The table is sharded across 64 GPUs, each holding ~16 GB. Training throughput is terrible — GPUs are 80% idle. The network isn't saturated. What's going on?

scenario: Your company is evaluating CXL-attached memory pools for ML inference. The pitch: 'Expand GPU-accessible memory to terabytes at DRAM cost.' Despite having ample physical memory, you observe very high CPU utilization but surprisingly low throughput, and profiling tools like `perf` show a sign

title: The Phantom Update
scenario: You are developing a high-performance, multi-threaded C++ training loop for a small MLP on a single server with multiple CPU cores. title: The Fragmentation Crisis
scenario: We are serving a chatbot. Even though we have 40GB of free VRAM, our inference server refuses to accept new concurrent requests, citing an 'Out of Memory' error. What is consuming our VRAM invisibly, and how do we fix it?

title: The Gradient Checkpointing Boundary
scenario: We need to train a 70B model on 8× H100 80 GB GPUs. The team enabled ZeRO-3 and claims it'll fit. Walk me through the actual per-GPU memory budget and tell me where it breaks.

title: The INT8 Throughput Advantage
scenario: Your colleague claims 'INT8 inference is always 2× faster than FP16 because the data is half the size.' On an H100 with dedicated INT8 Tensor Cores, is this claim correct? When is it wrong?

title: The GGUF Quantization Ladder
scenario: You're deploying Llama-3 70B on a single RTX 4090 (24 GB VRAM) using llama.cpp. You need to choose between Q4_K_M, Q5_K_M, and Q8_0 quantization. Walk me through the model size, throughput, and quality trade-offs for each — and tell me which ones actually fit.

title: The FP16 vs BF16 Question
scenario: A new hire asks you: 'FP16 and BF16 are both 16 bits. Why does everyone use BF16 for training? Aren't they the same?' Give them the 2-minute explanation.

title: The CUDA Upgrade Regression

title: The Quantization Noise Floor

title: The Model Compression Pipeline
scenario: You have a 70B FP16 model that costs \$1.20 per 1M tokens to serve on 4× H100 GPUs. Your target is \$0.40 per 1M tokens on a single H100. Design a compression pipeline that achieves this 3× cost reduction while keeping quality degradation under 2% on your evaluation suite.

title: The FP16 Divergence
scenario: We're pre-training a 13B model. It trains perfectly in BF16 for 100k steps. When we switch to FP16 with dynamic loss scaling, it diverges around step 50k with loss spikes and eventual NaNs. The hyperparameters are identical. What is happening at step 50k that wasn't happening at step 1k?

title: The Precision Trade-off

title: The Fine-Tuning Estimate
